html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 神异经 - Title of the text; can be translated as 'The Book of Strange and Marvelous Things' or 'The Classic of Wondrous Phenomena.'
  • 树主九州 - Phrase meaning 'the tree governs the nine regions.'
  • 枝主一州 - Phrase meaning 'each branch governs one region.'
  • 其高千丈，围百丈 - Description of the tree's height and circumference; 'a thousand feet tall' and 'a hundred feet in circumference.'
  • 本上三百丈 - Description of the trunk's height; 'the trunk rises three hundred feet.'
  • 本如有条枝，敷张如帐 - Description of the branches and leaves; 'branching limbs' and 'spread out like a tent.'
  • 上有玄狐黑猿 - Description of the animals on the tree; 'there are black foxes and black apes on it.'
  • 南北并列，面向西南 - Description of the branches' orientation; 'arranged north and south, facing southwest.'
  • 有九力士操斧伐之，以占九州吉凶 - Description of the nine strong men; 'there are nine strong men wielding axes to cut it, to predict the good or bad fortune of the nine regions.'
  • 斫复，其州有福 - Description of the tree's regrowth; 'if it is cut and regrows, the region will have good fortune.'
  • 迟者州伯有病 - Description of the tree's slow growth; 'if it grows slowly, the governor of the region will be ill.'
  • 积岁不复者，其州灭亡 - Description of the tree's failure to regrow; 'if it does not regrow after many years, the region will perish.'
  • 东方有桑树焉 - Description of the mulberry tree in the east; 'there is a mulberry tree in the east.'
  • 高八十丈，敷张自辅 - Description of the mulberry tree's height and spread; 'eighty feet high and spreads out to support itself.'
  • 其叶长一丈，广六七尺 - Description of the mulberry tree's leaves; 'a foot long and six or seven feet wide.'
  • 其上自有蚕，作茧长三尺，缲一茧，得丝一斤 - Description of the silkworms on the mulberry tree; 'the tree has its own silkworms, which produce cocoons three feet long, and one cocoon produces one pound of silk.'
  • 有椹焉，长三尺五寸，围如长 - Description of the mulberries; 'there are mulberries, which are three feet and five inches long, with a circumference as long as their length.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 神异经 - Attributed to the Han dynasty scholar Dongfang Shuo, known for his wit and wisdom.
  • 豫章 - A type of tree considered sacred in ancient China.
  • 九州 - The nine regions of ancient China, used for administrative purposes.
  • 玄狐黑猿 - Black foxes and black apes, considered auspicious or ominous depending on context.
  • 九力士 - Nine strong men, possibly representing the nine regions or a group of powerful figures.
  • 州伯 - The governor of a region, responsible for its administration and welfare.

Proper Nouns

  • 神异经 - Title of the text.
  • 豫章 - The type of tree described in the text.
  • 九州 - The nine regions of ancient China.
  • 玄狐黑猿 - Black foxes and black apes.
  • 九力士 - The nine strong men.
  • 州伯 - The governor of a region.
  • 桑树 - The mulberry tree.
  • 蚕 - Silkworms.
  • 椹 - Mulberries.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 荒外 - Refers to the outer regions or remote areas, often used in a mythological or fantastical context.
  • 不昼之木 - A literal phrase meaning 'wood that does not sleep,' likely referring to a tree that is always burning.
  • 昼夜火燃 - Means 'burning day and night,' emphasizing the continuous nature of the fire.
  • 得暴风不猛，猛雨不灭 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'it does not diminish in the face of strong winds, nor does it extinguish in heavy rain,' highlighting the tree's resilience.
  • 敷张自辅 - Means 'spread out and support itself,' describing the tree's expansive and self-sustaining nature.
  • 不畏水火，不畏白刃 - Means 'not afraid of water and fire, not afraid of sharp blades,' emphasizing the tree's invulnerability to natural and human threats.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 火山 - Refers to a volcano, a natural phenomenon often associated with mythological or fantastical elements in Chinese literature.
  • 祖、稼、■〈木匿〉 - Names of trees, with specific descriptions of their characteristics and growth cycles. 祖,柤梨也 (likely a pear tree); 稼,株稼也 (possibly a fruit-bearing tree); ■〈木匿〉,亲■〈木匿〉也 (likely a specific species).
  • 南方大荒 - Refers to the southern wilderness, a common setting in Chinese mythology for fantastical creatures and plants.
  • 地仙 - A concept from Chinese mythology, referring to immortals who live on earth.
  • 白刃 - Refers to sharp blades or weapons, often used metaphorically to represent danger or conflict.

Proper Nouns

  • 火山 - Volcano.
  • 祖、稼、■〈木匿〉 - Names of trees, with specific descriptions.
  • 如何 - Name of a tree, with specific characteristics.
  • 地仙 - Immortal living on earth.
  • 白刃 - Sharp blades or weapons.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 敷张自辅 - Means 'spreading and supporting itself,' describing the tree's expansive nature.
  • 辟谷 - Refers to the practice of abstaining from food, often associated with Daoist practices for longevity and spiritual cultivation.
  • 地仙 - Refers to a local immortal, a figure in Chinese mythology who achieves immortality through specific practices.
  • 无叶 - Another name for the tree, meaning 'leafless,' as later generations do not see the leaves, hence the name.
  • 倚骄 - Another name for the tree, meaning 'arrogant,' as the tree is so tall and straight that it cannot be reached.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 多罗 - A place name, possibly referring to a region known for its unique flora or people.
  • 张茂先 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or commentator, whose notes provide additional context.

Proper Nouns

  • 地仙 - Local immortal.
  • 辟谷 - Practice of abstaining from food.
  • 多罗 - Place name.
  • 张茂先 - Historical figure, possibly a scholar or commentator.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 名曰 - Formal way to name something.
  • 味和 - Means a pleasant or balanced taste.
  • 益寿 - Means to increase longevity.
  • 仁 - Refers to the kernel or seed.
  • 温润 - Means warm and moist.
  • 安躯 - Means to strengthen the body.
  • 减多益少 - Means to reduce the excess and increase the beneficial.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 赤松子 - The Red Pine Sage, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology known for his longevity and wisdom.
  • 北方大枣 - Northern dates, which are considered superior in quality and have special medicinal properties.
  • 南方荒中 - The southern wilderness, a region often associated with exotic and valuable resources in classical Chinese literature.
  • 邯■〈甘庶〉之林 - A grove of sugarcane, which is a valuable crop in southern China.
  • 创疠 - Sores and leprosy, conditions that were common and feared in ancient times.

Proper Nouns

  • 赤松子 - The Red Pine Sage.
  • 北方大枣 - Northern dates.
  • 南方荒中 - The southern wilderness.
  • 邯■〈甘庶〉之林 - A grove of sugarcane.
  • 创疠 - Sores and leprosy.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不昼木火中有鼠 - Refers to a nocturnal rat in a firewood environment.
  • 重千斤 - An exaggerated expression for a very heavy rat.
  • 毛长二尺余 - An exaggerated expression for exceptionally long hair.
  • 细如丝 - Describes the texture of the hair as fine as silk.
  • 但居火中洞赤 - Describes the rat's habit of living in fire and its reddish color.
  • 时时出外 - Describes the rat's behavior of occasionally leaving its habitat.
  • 而毛白 - Contrasts the rat's red body with its white hair.
  • 以水逐而沃之即死 - Describes the rat's vulnerability to water.
  • 取其毛绩纺织以为布 - Describes the use of the rat's hair for making fabric.
  • 用之若有垢涴 - Describes the fabric's tendency to get dirty.
  • 以火烧之则净也 - Describes how to clean the fabric.
  • 北方层冰万里 - Refers to the vast icy landscape in the north.
  • 厚百丈 - Emphasizes the thickness of the ice.
  • 有磎鼠在水下土中焉 - Refers to a specific type of rat that lives in water and soil.
  • 形如鼠 - Describes the rat's appearance as similar to a common rat.
  • 食草木 - Describes the rat's diet as feeding on grass and trees.
  • 肉重千斤 - An exaggerated expression for a very heavy rat.
  • 可以作脯 - Describes the use of the rat's flesh for food.
  • 食之已热 - Describes the medicinal properties of the rat's flesh.
  • 其毛八尺 - An exaggerated expression for exceptionally long hair.
  • 可以为褥 - Describes the use of the rat's hair for bedding.
  • 卧之却寒 - Describes the warmth of the cushion.
  • 其皮可以蒙鼓 - Describes the use of the rat's skin for musical instruments.
  • 闻千里 - Describes the loudness of the drum.
  • 其毛可以来鼠 - Describes the rat's hair's ability to attract other rats.
  • 此尾所在鼠聚 - Describes the rat's tail's ability to attract other rats.
  • 今江南鼠 - Refers to the current situation of rats in southern China.
  • 食草木为灾 - Describes the destructive behavior of the rats.
  • 西方山中有蛇 - Refers to the presence of snakes in western mountains.
  • 头尾差大 - Describes the snake's body structure as having a slightly larger head and tail.
  • 有色五彩 - Describes the snake's colorful appearance.
  • 人物触之者 - Refers to people and things that touch the snake.
  • 中头则尾至 - Describes the snake's reaction to being touched on the head.
  • 中尾则头至 - Describes the snake's reaction to being touched on the tail.
  • 中腰则头尾并至 - Describes the snake's reaction to being touched on the waist.
  • 名曰率然 - Refers to the name of the snake as *Shuoran*.
  • 张茂先注曰 - Refers to the commentary by Zhang Maoxian.
  • 会稽常山最多此蛇 - Indicates the snake's habitat in Kuaiji and Changshan.
  • 故孙子兵法“三军势如率然者”是也 - Refers to the metaphorical use of the snake in military strategy.
  • 北海有大鸟 - Refers to the presence of a large bird in the north sea.
  • 其高千尺 - Describes the bird's size as having a height of a thousand feet.
  • 头文曰天 - Describes the bird's head marking as the character *Tian*.
  • 胸文曰侯 - Describes the bird's chest marking as the character *Hou*.
  • 左翼丈曰鷖 - Describes the bird's left wing marking as the character *Yi*.
  • 右翼文曰勒 - Describes the bird's right wing marking as the character *Le*.
  • 头向东正 - Describes the bird's orientation as facing east.
  • 海中央捕鱼 - Describes the bird's activity as fishing in the middle of the sea.
  • 或时举翼而飞 - Describes the bird's flight behavior as sometimes raising its wings and flying.
  • 其羽相切如风雷也 - Describes the sound of the bird's flight as its feathers brushing together like wind and thunder.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 不昼木火中有鼠 - Refers to a specific type of rat that lives in firewood and is nocturnal.
  • 重千斤 - An exaggerated expression for a very heavy rat, emphasizing its unusual size.
  • 毛长二尺余 - An exaggerated expression for exceptionally long hair, emphasizing the rat's unique physical feature.
  • 细如丝 - A descriptive phrase for the texture of the hair, indicating its fine quality.
  • 但居火中洞赤 - Describes the rat's habit of living in fire and its reddish color, emphasizing its unique habitat.
  • 时时出外 - Describes the rat's behavior of occasionally leaving its habitat, indicating its activity pattern.
  • 而毛白 - Contrasts the rat's red body with its white hair, emphasizing the color difference.
  • 以水逐而沃之即死 - Describes the rat's vulnerability to water, indicating its weakness.
  • 取其毛绩纺织以为布 - Describes the use of the rat's hair for making fabric, indicating its practical application.
  • 用之若有垢涴 - Describes the fabric's tendency to get dirty, indicating its maintenance requirement.
  • 以火烧之则净也 - Describes how to clean the fabric, indicating its cleaning method.

  • 可以作脯 - Describes the use of the rat's flesh for food, indicating its culinary value.
  • 食之已热 - Describes the medicinal properties of the rat's flesh, indicating its health benefits.
  • 其毛八尺 - An exaggerated expression for exceptionally long hair, emphasizing the rat's unique physical feature.
  • 可以为褥 - Describes the use of the rat's hair for bedding, indicating its practical application.
  • 卧之却寒 - Describes the warmth of the cushion, indicating its comfort.
  • 其皮可以蒙鼓 - Describes the use of the rat's skin for musical instruments, indicating its practical application.
  • 闻千里 - Describes the loudness of the drum, indicating its sound quality.
  • 其毛可以来鼠 - Describes the rat's hair's ability to attract other rats, indicating its unique property.
  • 此尾所在鼠聚 - Describes the rat's tail's ability to attract other rats.
  • 今江南鼠 - Refers to the current situation of rats in southern China.
  • 食草木为灾 - Describes the destructive behavior of the rats.
  • 西方山中有蛇 - Refers to the presence of snakes in western mountains.
  • 头尾差大 - Describes the snake's body structure as having a slightly larger head and tail.
  • 有色五彩 - Describes the snake's colorful appearance.
  • 人物触之者 - Refers to people and things that touch the snake.
  • 中头则尾至 - Describes the snake's reaction to being touched on the head.
  • 中尾则头至 - Describes the snake's reaction to being touched on the tail.
  • 中腰则头尾并至 - Describes the snake's reaction to being touched on the waist.
  • 名曰率然 - Refers to the name of the snake as *Shuoran*.
  • 张茂先注曰 - Refers to the commentary by Zhang Maoxian.
  • 会稽常山最多此蛇 - Indicates the snake's habitat in Kuaiji and Changshan.

  • 其高千尺 - Describes the bird's size as having a height of a thousand feet.
  • 头文曰天 - Describes the bird's head marking as the character *Tian*.
  • 胸文曰侯 - Describes the bird's chest marking as the character *Hou*.
  • 左翼丈曰鷖 - Describes the bird's left wing marking as the character *Yi*.
  • 右翼文曰勒 - Describes the bird's right wing marking as the character *Le*.
  • 头向东正 - Describes the bird's orientation as facing east.
  • 海中央捕鱼 - Describes the bird's activity as fishing in the middle of the sea.
  • 或时举翼而飞 - Describes the bird's flight behavior as sometimes raising its wings and flying.
  • 其羽相切如风雷也 - Describes the sound of the bird's flight as its feathers brushing together like wind and thunder.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 恒冰 - Means 'perpetually frozen.'
  • 刺之不入，煮之不死 - Describes the fish's supernatural properties of being impervious to stabbing and boiling.
  • 以乌梅二枚煮之则熟 - Specifies that cooking the fish with two pieces of plum makes it edible.
  • 食之可止邪病 - Suggests that eating the fish can cure evil diseases.
  • 目明者见之 - Indicates that only those with keen eyesight can see these insects.
  • 蜚而俱去，蚊遂不知 - Describes the insects flying away together and mosquitoes being unaware of their presence.
  • 亦食人及百兽 - States that the insects also feed on humans and animals.
  • 食者知言虫小食人不去也 - Explains that people who eat the insects know that despite their small size, they do not leave the body.
  • 此虫既细且小 - Describes the insects as both small and tiny.
  • 因曰细蠛 - Names the insects '细蠛' (xìmài), meaning 'tiny and minute.'
  • 陈章对齐桓公小虫是也 - Refers to a historical anecdote where a small insect was mentioned by Chen Zhang to Duke Huan of Qi.
  • 此虫常春生，以季夏藏于鹿耳中 - States that the insects are born in spring and hide in deer ears during late summer.
  • 名婴蜺 - Names the insects '婴蜺' (yīngní), meaning 'infant and tiny.'
  • 八荒之中有毛人焉 - Refers to a remote and desolate region with hairy people.
  • 长七八尺 - Describes the hairy people as being about seven to eight feet tall.
  • 皆如人形 - Indicates that they resemble humans.
  • 身及头上，皆有毛 - Describes their entire body and head covered with hair.
  • 如猕猴 - Compares their appearance to that of a macaque.
  • ■〈生毛〉■〈丽毛〉 - Refers to their hair as being lush and beautiful.
  • 见人则瞑目，开口吐舌 - Describes their behavior of closing their eyes and sticking out their tongues when they see people.
  • 上唇覆面，下唇覆胸 - Indicates that their upper lip covers their face and their lower lip covers their chest.
  • 喜食人 - States that they enjoy eating humans.
  • 舌鼻牵引共戏 - Describes their playful behavior of pulling people's tongues and noses.
  • 不与即去 - Indicates that if they are not allowed to play, they will leave.
  • 名曰髯公，俗曰髯丽 - Names the hairy people '髯公' (rán gōng) and '髯丽' (rán lì), meaning 'bearded man' and 'beautiful bearded man.'
  • 一名髯狎小儿 - Another name for them is '髯狎小儿' (rán xiá xiǎo ér), meaning 'bearded playful child.'
  • 髯狎，可畏也 - Suggests that they are to be feared due to their playful and potentially harmful nature.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 横公鱼 - A mythical fish found in ancient Chinese folklore, often associated with supernatural properties.
  • 乌梅 - Refers to plums, which are used in traditional Chinese medicine and cuisine.
  • 陈章对齐桓公小虫是也 - Refers to a historical anecdote involving Chen Zhang and Duke Huan of Qi, where a small insect was mentioned.

Proper Nouns

  • 横公鱼 - The mythical fish.
  • 乌梅 - Plums.
  • 陈章 - A historical figure.
  • 齐桓公 - Duke Huan of Qi, a historical ruler.
  • 髯公 - A name for the hairy people.
  • 髯丽 - Another name for the hairy people.
  • 髯狎小儿 - Another name for the hairy people.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鹄国 - A mythical country inhabited by small people.
  • 自然有礼 - Naturally polite.
  • 好经编跪拜 - Enjoy performing rituals like kneeling and bowing.
  • 一举千里 - Fly a thousand miles in one leap.
  • 玉馈之酒 - Wine made of jade.
  • 酒泉 - A spring of wine.
  • 玉尊玉笾 - Jade cups and plates.
  • 与天同休 - Last as long as heaven.
  • 无乾时 - Never run out.
  • 西荒 - The far west.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 西北荒中 - Refers to the remote northwest, often used in classical texts to describe distant and mysterious lands.
  • 小人 - In this context, it refers to a small person, not to be confused with the modern derogatory term.
  • 朱衣玄冠 - Describes a red robe and black cap, typical attire for a ruler or dignitary.
  • 乘辂车马 - Refers to riding in a grand carriage, symbolizing authority and status.
  • 引为威仪 - Means to use as a symbol of dignity and respect.
  • 抓而食之 - Literally 'grab and eat,' referring to the act of consuming something quickly or without hesitation.
  • 辛 - Means pungent or spicy, describing the taste.
  • 终年不为（阙）所咋并识万物名字 - The phrase is incomplete due to a missing character, but it likely means 'throughout the year, not affected by (something) and knowing the names of all things.'
  • 杀腹中三虫 - Refers to killing three worms inside the body, often associated with health and longevity practices.
  • 食仙药 - Means to eat elixirs of immortality, a common theme in Daoist and alchemical texts.
  • 南有人焉 - Refers to someone in the south, often used to describe mythical or extraordinary beings.
  • 周行天下 - Means to travel throughout the world, indicating a wide-ranging journey.
  • 朱衣缟带 - Describes a red robe and white belt, typical attire for a dignified figure.
  • 赤蛇绕其项 - Refers to a red snake coiling around the neck, a symbol of power or protection.
  • 朝吞恶鬼三千，暮吞三百 - Literally 'swallowing 3,000 evil spirits in the morning and 300 in the evening,' indicating a powerful ability to consume malevolent forces.
  • 以鬼为饭，以露为浆 - Means to use ghosts as food and dew as drink, emphasizing a supernatural diet.
  • 名曰尺郭，一名黄父 - Refers to the names 'Chige' and 'Huangfu,' which are likely titles or names for the figure.
  • 西方曰宫之外有山焉 - Describes a mountain outside the palace in the west, often used to describe a sacred or significant location.
  • 大黄之金 - Refers to a type of gold, possibly a specific mineral or alloy.
  • 色殊美 - Means exceptionally beautiful in color.
  • 不杂土石 - Means not mixed with earth or stones, indicating purity.
  • 不生草木 - Means no plants grow there, emphasizing the unique nature of the place.
  • 金人 - Refers to golden figures or statues.
  • 丹阳铜 - Refers to a type of copper, possibly a specific mineral or alloy.
  • 似金可锻 - Means it looks like gold and can be forged, indicating its value and workability.
  • 以作错涂之器也 - Means to make it into a polished and decorated object, emphasizing its aesthetic and practical uses.
  • 西荒之中有人焉 - Refers to someone in the remote west, often used to describe mythical or extraordinary beings.
  • 著败衣 - Means wearing tattered clothes, indicating poverty or simplicity.
  • 手虎爪 - Refers to having tiger claws, indicating strength or ferocity.
  • 伺人独行 - Means waiting for someone to walk alone, indicating a predatory behavior.
  • 辄食人脑 - Means to immediately eat human brains, indicating a violent and cannibalistic nature.
  • 舌出盘地丈馀 - Means the tongue extends over a distance of more than one zhang (about 3.3 meters), indicating a large and dangerous tongue.
  • 人先闻声 - Means people hear the sound first, indicating a warning before the attack.
  • 烧大石以投其舌 - Means to throw a heated stone at the tongue, indicating a method to neutralize the threat.
  • 气绝而死 - Means to die from lack of breath, indicating the effectiveness of the method.
  • 不然食人脑矣 - Means otherwise, they would eat human brains, indicating the danger if not stopped.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西北荒中 - Often used in classical texts to describe distant and mysterious lands, sometimes associated with mythological or supernatural beings.
  • 小人 - In this context, it refers to a small person, not to be confused with the modern derogatory term. It could be a mythological or legendary figure.
  • 朱衣玄冠 - Typical attire for a ruler or dignitary, symbolizing authority and status.
  • 乘辂车马 - Symbolizes authority and status, often associated with high-ranking officials or rulers.
  • 引为威仪 - Symbolizes dignity and respect, often used in ceremonial contexts.
  • 抓而食之 - A common motif in classical literature, often associated with mythical creatures or beings with unusual abilities.
  • 辛 - Describes the pungent or spicy taste, often used to describe the flavor of certain foods or herbs.
  • 终年不为（阙）所咋并识万物名字 - Likely refers to a state of being unaffected by certain conditions and having extensive knowledge, possibly a reference to a sage or enlightened being.
  • 杀腹中三虫 - A common theme in Daoist and alchemical texts, often associated with health and longevity practices.
  • 食仙药 - A common theme in Daoist and alchemical texts, often associated with immortality and spiritual transformation.
  • 南有人焉 - Often used to describe mythical or extraordinary beings, sometimes associated with magical or supernatural powers.
  • 周行天下 - Often used to describe a wide-ranging journey, sometimes associated with a quest for knowledge or enlightenment.
  • 朱衣缟带 - Typical attire for a dignified figure, often associated with high moral or spiritual status.
  • 赤蛇绕其项 - A symbol of power or protection, often associated with mythical or legendary figures.
  • 朝吞恶鬼三千，暮吞三百 - A motif often used to describe a powerful ability to consume malevolent forces, sometimes associated with protective or purifying deities.
  • 以鬼为饭，以露为浆 - A motif often used to describe a supernatural diet, sometimes associated with immortality or spiritual transformation.
  • 名曰尺郭，一名黄父 - Likely titles or names for the figure, possibly associated with mythological or legendary beings.
  • 西方曰宫之外有山焉 - Often used to describe a sacred or significant location, sometimes associated with hidden treasures or mystical places.
  • 大黄之金 - A type of gold, possibly a specific mineral or alloy, often associated with value and rarity.
  • 色殊美 - Describes the exceptional beauty in color, often used to emphasize the uniqueness of a place or object.
  • 不杂土石 - Emphasizes the purity of the place, often used to describe a sacred or significant location.
  • 不生草木 - Emphasizes the unique nature of the place, often used to describe a sacred or significant location.
  • 金人 - Golden figures or statues, often associated with wealth, power, or spiritual significance.
  • 丹阳铜 - A type of copper, possibly a specific mineral or alloy, often associated with value and workability.
  • 似金可锻 - Emphasizes the value and workability of the material, often used to describe precious metals.
  • 以作错涂之器也 - Emphasizes the aesthetic and practical uses of the material, often used to describe valuable objects.
  • 西荒之中有人焉 - Often used to describe mythical or extraordinary beings, sometimes associated with magical or supernatural powers.
  • 著败衣 - Indicates poverty or simplicity, often used to describe a humble or unassuming figure.
  • 手虎爪 - Indicates strength or ferocity, often used to describe a dangerous or predatory being.
  • 伺人独行 - Indicates a predatory behavior, often used to describe a dangerous or malevolent being.
  • 辄食人脑 - Indicates a violent and cannibalistic nature, often used to describe a dangerous or malevolent being.
  • 舌出盘地丈馀 - Indicates a large and dangerous tongue, often used to describe a fearsome or threatening being.
  • 人先闻声 - Indicates a warning before the attack, often used to describe a dangerous or threatening being.
  • 烧大石以投其舌 - Indicates a method to neutralize the threat, often used to describe a practical or effective solution.
  • 气绝而死 - Indicates the effectiveness of the method, often used to describe a successful defense against danger.
  • 不然食人脑矣 - Indicates the danger if not stopped, often used to describe the consequences of failing to defend against a threat.

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 南荒之外有火山，昼夜火燃 - Literally means 'beyond the southern wilderness, there is a volcano that burns day and night,' describing a natural phenomenon.
  • 火中有鼠重百斤，毛长二尺余，细如丝，可以作布 - Describes a mouse in the fire that weighs a hundred pounds, with fur two feet long, fine as silk, and capable of being made into cloth.
  • 恒居火中，时时出外而白，以水逐而沃之乃死，取其毛缉织以为布 - The mouse constantly lives in the fire, occasionally coming out to cool off, and can only be killed by pouring water on it. Its fur can be used to make cloth.
  • 南方之兽如鹿豕头，善依人求五穀，名无损兽 - Describes a southern beast with a deer-like head, good at following people to ask for grains, and named '无损兽' (non-damaging beast).
  • 人割取肉不病，肉自复，其肉惟可作鮓，使■〈米审〉肥美，而咋肉不坏，吞之不入 - The meat can be cut without causing illness, and it regenerates. It can only be made into pickled meat, which makes the ■〈米审〉 (a type of bird) fat and delicious, but eating the meat does not digest.
  • ■〈米审〉尽更澡肉，复作鮓如初，愈美，名不尽鮓是也 - After the ■〈米审〉 finishes eating, the meat regenerates, and the pickled meat can be made again, even more delicious, hence the name '不尽鮓' (unending pickled meat).
  • 西南荒中出讹兽，其状若菟，人面能言 - Describes a beast in the southwestern wilderness that looks like a rabbit, has a human face, and can speak.
  • 常欺人，言东而西，言恶而善 - The beast often deceives people, saying east when it means west, and saying evil when it means good.
  • 其肉美，食之言不真矣 - The meat is delicious, but eating it makes one's words unreliable.
  • 东海之外荒海中，有山焦炎而峙，高深莫测，盖禀至阳之为质也 - Describes a mountain in the eastern sea that is fiery and stands tall, with height and depth that cannot be measured, and is said to be made of pure yang energy.
  • 海水激浪投其上，噏然而尽，计其昼夜嗡摄无极，若熬鼎受其洒汗耳 - The sea water, when thrown onto the mountain, is absorbed instantly, and the amount of water absorbed day and night is immeasurable, like a pot being filled with sweat.
  • 大荒之东极至鬼府山臂沃椒山脚巨洋海中升载海曰，盖扶桑山 - Describes a place in the easternmost part of the vast wilderness, near the foot of Mount Wuoji, where the giant ocean rises, known as Mount Fusang.
  • 有玉鸡，王鸡鸣则金鸡鸣，金鸡鸣则石鸡鸣，石鸡鸣则天下之鸡鸣，悉鸣则潮水应之矣 - Describes a jade chicken, a king chicken, a gold chicken, and a stone chicken, where the sound of each chicken triggers the next, and when all chickens crow, the tides respond.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 南荒 - Refers to the southern wilderness, a region beyond the known world.
  • 无损兽 - A mythical beast that does not harm humans.
  • 讹兽 - A deceptive beast, often used in classical texts to warn against deceit.
  • 东海 - The Eastern Sea, often associated with mythical places in Chinese mythology.
  • 扶桑山 - A mythical mountain in the East, often associated with the sun and the land of the gods.

Proper Nouns

  • 无损兽 - A mythical beast that does not harm humans.
  • 讹兽 - A deceptive beast.
  • 扶桑山 - A mythical mountain in the East.

[10]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 西南大荒 - Refers to the remote southwestern regions, often used in ancient texts to describe distant and mythical lands.
  • 長一丈，其腹圍九尺 - Describes a person of extraordinary size, with a height of one zhang (approximately 3.3 meters) and a waist circumference of nine chi (approximately 2.7 meters).
  • 踐龜蛇，戴朱鳥 - Metaphorically describes the person standing on turtles and snakes and wearing a red bird, symbolizing divine or supernatural attributes.
  • 右手憑青龍，左手憑白虎 - Describes the person leaning on the Azure Dragon and the White Tiger, which are symbols of the East and West, respectively, in Chinese cosmology.
  • 知河海斗斛，識山石多少 - Means the person has knowledge of the measurements of rivers and seas, and the quantity of mountains and stones.
  • 知天下鳥獸言語，知百穀草木鹽苦 - Indicates the person understands the languages of birds and beasts, and the flavors of grains, grasses, and plants.
  • 名曰聖，一名哲，一名賢，一名無不達 - Lists the various titles given to the person, emphasizing their wisdom and omniscience.
  • 凡人見而拜者，令人神智 - Suggests that anyone who sees and worships the person will become more intelligent and spiritually enlightened.
  • 西北海外 - Refers to the remote western regions, often associated with mythical or legendary places.
  • 長二千里，兩脚中间相去千里，腹圍一千六百里 - Describes a person of immense size, with a height of two thousand li (approximately 667 kilometers), a distance of one thousand li between their feet, and a waist circumference of one thousand six hundred li (approximately 533 kilometers).
  • 但曰飲天酒五斗 - Means the person only drinks five dou (approximately 6 liters) of Tianjiu (Heavenly Wine), which is a metaphor for the sweet dew or nectar of the heavens.
  • 好游山海間，不犯百姓，不干万物 - Describes the person as enjoying traveling through mountains and seas, not harming ordinary people, and not interfering with the natural world.
  • 與天地同生，名无路之人，一名仁，一名信，一名神 - Lists the various titles given to the person, emphasizing their divine and virtuous nature.
  • 西荒中兽如虎 - Describes a creature resembling a tiger, with specific physical characteristics.
  • 豪长三尺 - Refers to the creature's whiskers or hair being three chi (approximately 0.9 meters) long.
  • 人面虎足口牙一丈八尺 - Describes the creature having a human face, tiger feet, and a mouth and teeth measuring one zhang eight chi (approximately 6 meters).
  • 人或食之，兽斗终不退却，唯死而已 - Indicates that if someone eats the creature, it will fight to the death without retreating.
  • 荒中人张捕之，复黠逆知 - Describes how people in the remote areas hunt the creature, using cunning and foresight.
  • 一名倒寿焉 - Gives the creature another name, '倒寿' (Dàoshòu), which can be translated as 'Reversed Lifespan.'
  • 东方外有东明山 - Refers to a mountain located to the east, known as Dongming Mountain.
  • 有宫焉，左右有阙而立 - Describes the palace with two gate towers standing on either side.
  • 其高百尺，画以五色，青石为墙 - Describes the palace's height, color, and construction materials.
  • 高三仞，门有银榜，以青石碧镂 - Describes the height of the walls, the silver plaque on the door, and the blue stone with green carvings.
  • 题曰“天地长男之宫” - The plaque reads 'The Palace of the Eldest Son of Heaven and Earth.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西南大荒 - Refers to the mythical and remote southwestern regions, often associated with legends and supernatural beings.
  • 西北海外 - Refers to the mythical and remote western regions, often associated with legends and supernatural beings.
  • 天酒 - Refers to the sweet dew or nectar of the heavens, a metaphor for divine sustenance.
  • 东明山 - A mythical mountain located to the east, often associated with divine or celestial significance.
  • 天地长男之宫 - Refers to the palace of the eldest son of Heaven and Earth, a metaphor for a divine or supreme being.

Proper Nouns

  • 西南大荒 - The remote southwestern regions.
  • 西北海外 - The remote western regions.
  • 天酒 - Heavenly Wine, a metaphor for divine sustenance.
  • 东明山 - Dongming Mountain, a mythical mountain.
  • 天地长男之宫 - The Palace of the Eldest Son of Heaven and Earth, a metaphor for a divine or supreme being.

[11]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 西北荒有遗酒，追复脯焉 - Describes a place in the northwest where wine and dried meat can be found. '追复' is an unusual term, possibly indicating a method or process related to the preparation of the meat.
  • 其味如鹿獐，食一斤复一斤 - Describes the taste of the meat being similar to that of deer or gazelle, and suggests a peculiar property where eating one pound leads to craving another pound.
  • 东南荒中邪木，高二三十丈 - Refers to a strange tree in the southeast that grows to a height of twenty to thirty zhang.
  • 叶如甘瓜，子如寒瓜，蔓覆其顶，子味甘如蜜 - Describes the leaves of the tree as resembling sweet melons, the fruits as resembling cold melons, and the vines covering the top of the tree, with the fruits tasting as sweet as honey.
  • 不可过三升，令人冥醉，半曰乃醒 - Warns that consuming more than three sheng of the fruit will cause a deep intoxication that lasts half a day.
  • 惟多罗氏之民缘能得之 - Indicates that only the people of the Doro clan can obtain this fruit.
  • 东海沧浪洲生强木焉，洲人多用作舟楫 - Describes a strong wood growing on the island of Canglang in the East Sea, which is commonly used by the islanders for making boats.
  • 其上多以珠玉为戏，物终无所负 - Suggests that the wood is often used for games involving jewels and jade, and that despite its value, it does not carry any negative consequences.
  • 其木方一寸，可载百许斤，纵石镇之，不能没矣 - Describes the strength of the wood, stating that a piece one inch square can support up to a hundred pounds and will not sink even if weighed down with stones.
  • 木梨生南方，梨径三尺，剖之少瓤白素 - Describes a large pear-like fruit growing in the south, with a diameter of three chi and a small amount of white, pure flesh inside.
  • 和羹食之地仙，可以水火不焦溺矣 - Suggests that the fruit is so pure that it can be used in soups and stews, and that those who eat it can become immortals who are unaffected by fire and water.
  • 木栗出东北荒中，有木高四十丈，叶长五尺，广三寸，名栗 - Describes a tall tree in the northeast that produces a type of chestnut, with leaves that are five chi long and three cun wide.
  • 其实径三尺二寸，其壳赤，其肉黄白，味甜，食之令人短气而渴也 - Describes the fruit as having a diameter of three chi two cun, a red shell, and yellowish-white flesh that is sweet but causes shortness of breath and thirst when eaten.
  • 昆仑山上有柰，冬生子，碧色，须玉井水洗之方可食也 - Describes a type of fruit growing on Mount Kunlun that ripens in winter, has a green color, and must be washed with water from the Jade Well before it can be eaten.
  • 刀味核生南荒中，树形高五十丈，实如枣，长五尺 - Describes a tree in the south that is fifty zhang tall and produces fruit resembling dates, each five chi long.
  • 金刀剖之则甜，若竹刀剖之则饴，木刀剖之则酸，芦刀剖之则辛 - Describes how the fruit tastes differently depending on the type of knife used to cut it: sweet with a gold knife, sticky with a bamboo knife, sour with a wooden knife, and pungent with a reed knife.
  • 食之地仙，不畏水火白刃 - Suggests that those who eat the fruit can become immortals who are not afraid of water, fire, or sharp weapons.
  • 东南隅大荒中有朴父焉，其高千里，腹围自辅 - Describes a giant tree in the southeast that is a thousand li tall and has a circumference of its trunk that is self-supporting.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西北荒 - Refers to a remote and desolate region in the northwest, often associated with mythical or legendary places in Chinese literature.
  • 东南荒 - Refers to a remote and desolate region in the southeast, similarly associated with mythical or legendary places.
  • 东海沧浪洲 - Refers to the island of Canglang in the East Sea, a place often mentioned in Chinese mythology and literature.
  • 昆仑山 - Refers to Mount Kunlun, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology known for its association with immortality and divine beings.
  • 玉井水 - Refers to water from the Jade Well, a mythical well often associated with purity and immortality in Chinese mythology.
  • 多罗氏 - Refers to the Doro clan, a mythical group of people often mentioned in ancient Chinese texts.
  • 地仙 - Refers to immortals who live on earth, a concept found in Chinese mythology and Taoist beliefs.

[12]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 西方深山中有山臊 - Literal description of a mythical creature called '山臊' (shān sào) living in a deep western mountain.
  • 犯人则病 - Means 'when it attacks people, they fall ill.'
  • 长爆竹声 - Refers to making loud firecracker noises to scare away the creature.
  • 西北裔外有大夏山 - Describes a mountain called '大夏山' (dà xià shān) located beyond the northwest border.
  • 有宫，以金为墙 - Means 'there is a palace with walls made of gold.'
  • 南方裔外冈明山 - Describes a mountain called '冈明山' (gāng míng shān) located beyond the southern border.
  • 有宫，以赤石为墙 - Means 'there is a palace with walls made of red stone.'
  • 西南裔外老寿山 - Describes a mountain called '老寿山' (lǎo shòu shān) located beyond the southwest border.
  • 有宫，以黄铜为墙 - Means 'there is a palace with walls made of yellow copper.'
  • 东南裔外阓清山 - Describes a mountain called '阓清山' (huì qīng shān) located beyond the southeast border.
  • 有宫，以青石为墙 - Means 'there is a palace with walls made of green stone.'
  • 西方裔外西明山 - Describes a mountain called '西明山' (xī míng shān) located beyond the western border.
  • 有宫，以白石为墙 - Means 'there is a palace with walls made of white stone.'
  • 北方异国有银盘 - Refers to a silver plate in a foreign land to the north.
  • 大五十丈 - Means 'large, fifty zhang in size' (a unit of measurement).
  • 中有明珠 - Means 'inside there is a pearl.'
  • 大数丈 - Means 'several zhang in size.'
  • 照千里 - Means 'illuminates for a thousand li' (a unit of distance).
  • 穷其兽似牛 - Describes an animal that resembles a cow.
  • 而色狸 - Means 'with the color of a fox.'
  • 尾长曳地 - Means 'with a tail that drags on the ground.'
  • 其声似狗 - Means 'its sound is like a dog.'
  • 狗头人形 - Means 'with a dog's head and human form.'
  • 钩爪锯牙 - Means 'with hooked claws and saw-like teeth.'
  • 逢忠信之人啮而食之 - Means 'when it encounters a loyal and trustworthy person, it bites and eats them.'
  • 逢奸邪者则擒禽兽而饲之 - Means 'when it encounters a wicked person, it captures animals and feeds them to them.'
  • 迅疾亦食诸禽兽也 - Means 'it also quickly eats various animals.'
  • 东方荒中有木 - Describes a tree in the eastern wilderness.
  • 名曰栗 - Means 'named '栗'.'
  • 有壳径三尺三寸 - Means 'with a shell diameter of three feet and three inches.'
  • 壳刺长丈余 - Means 'with spikes on the shell that are more than one zhang long.'
  • 实径三尺 - Means 'with a fruit diameter of three feet.'
  • 壳亦黄 - Means 'the shell is also yellow.'
  • 其味甜 - Means 'its taste is sweet.'
  • 食之多 - Means 'if eaten in large quantities.'
  • 令人短气而渴 - Means 'it causes one to feel short of breath and thirsty.'
  • 东南海中有烜洲 - Describes an island called '烜洲' (xuǎn zhōu) in the southeastern sea.
  • 洲有温湖 - Means 'the island has a warm lake.'
  • 鳐鱼生焉 - Means 'where the ray fish lives.'
  • 长八尺 - Means 'eight chi long' (a unit of length).
  • 食之宜暑 - Means 'eating it is suitable for hot weather.'
  • 辟风寒 - Means 'prevents wind and cold.'
  • 北方有石湖 - Refers to a stone lake in the north.
  • 其水恒赤 - Means 'the water is always red.'
  • 西北荒中有二金阙 - Describes two golden palaces in the northwest wilderness.
  • 相去百丈 - Means 'separated by a hundred zhang.'
  • 有明月珠 - Means 'there is a pearl called '明月珠'.'
  • 径二尺 - Means 'with a diameter of two feet.'
  • 光照二千里 - Means 'its light illuminates for two thousand li.'
  • 东方裔外有建山 - Describes a mountain called '建山' (jiàn shān) located beyond the eastern border.
  • 其上多橘柚 - Means 'on top of which there are many tangerines and pomelos.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 山臊 - A mythical creature in Chinese folklore, often depicted as a small, mischievous spirit that inhabits mountains and causes illness when encountered.
  • 爆竹 - Firecrackers, traditionally used during Chinese New Year to ward off evil spirits.
  • 大夏山 - A mythical mountain, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical location.
  • 金为墙 - Gold walls, symbolizing wealth and luxury in ancient Chinese culture.
  • 冈明山 - A mythical mountain, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical location.
  • 赤石 - Red stone, often associated with fire and passion in Chinese culture.
  • 老寿山 - A mythical mountain, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical location.
  • 黄铜 - Yellow copper, often associated with wealth and durability.
  • 阓清山 - A mythical mountain, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical location.
  • 青石 - Green stone, often associated with nature and tranquility.
  • 西明山 - A mythical mountain, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical location.
  • 白石 - White stone, often associated with purity and clarity.
  • 银盘 - Silver plate, symbolizing wealth and luxury.
  • 明珠 - Pearl, symbolizing beauty and value.
  • 穷其兽 - A mythical creature, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical animal.
  • 栗 - A type of tree, often associated with sweetness and abundance.
  • 烜洲 - A mythical island, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical location.
  • 温湖 - Warm lake, symbolizing comfort and relaxation.
  • 鳐鱼 - Ray fish, a type of marine creature.
  • 石湖 - Stone lake, possibly referring to a natural or artificial body of water.
  • 明月珠 - Pearl called '明月珠', symbolizing beauty and value.
  • 建山 - A mythical mountain, possibly referring to a legendary or fantastical location.
  • 橘柚 - Tangerines and pomelos, fruits often associated with abundance and prosperity.
